title: Transpiration & translocation — how plants move water and food
examTip: AQA loves graph questions on transpiration rate. Know exactly what each factor does and why. Distinguish transpiration (water, xylem) from translocation (sugars, phloem) — mixing them up is the most common dropped mark.
introTips: This lesson explains how a plant pulls water up from its roots to its leaves — no heart, no pump, just physics and clever leaf design.
You'll learn the four factors that speed up or slow down water loss, and be able to read or sketch graphs for each one.
You'll also nail the difference between transpiration and translocation — two words that look similar but describe completely different things.
intro: Plants have a problem: they need water at the top (for photosynthesis in the leaves) but water is at the bottom (in the soil). They can't pump it like your heart pumps blood. Instead, they use a trick: water evaporates out of the leaves, and that loss pulls more water up from the roots. It's called the transpiration stream — and understanding it will get you marks on almost every AQA Biology paper.

diagrams.title: The Transpiration Stream — root to leaf
Xylem and Phloem — structure and direction

keyPoints.heading: The transpiration stream — how water gets from root to leaf
keyPoints.content: Water enters the plant through root hair cells by osmosis. It then moves up through the xylem — long hollow tubes that run the full height of the plant. What pulls it up? Water evaporates from the leaves through tiny pores called stomata. As water leaves at the top, it pulls more water up behind it — like a chain. This continuous movement of water from roots up through the xylem and out of the leaves is called the transpiration stream. The xylem is made of dead cells: no living contents, just hollow tubes — perfectly designed to carry water.
writeBullets: Water enters root hair cells by osmosis
Water moves up the plant through the xylem (hollow dead cells)
Water evaporates from leaves through stomata
Water loss at the top pulls water up from the roots — this is the transpiration stream
Xylem carries water and dissolved mineral ions upward only

keyTerms.def: The evaporation of water from a plant's leaves through the stomata
The continuous movement of water from roots, up through the xylem, and out of the leaves
Tissue made of dead hollow cells that carries water and mineral ions up a plant
Tiny pores (mainly on the underside of leaves) through which gas exchange and water loss occur

keyPoints.memTip: Picture pulling a string of beads upward: as the top bead (water molecule) leaves through the stomata, it drags the next bead up. That chain is the transpiration stream. Sketch root → xylem → leaf → stomata with an arrow going up, and label it. The act of drawing it fixes the direction.

keyPoints.heading: Four factors that affect transpiration rate
keyPoints.content: Four things change how fast water evaporates from leaves — and AQA will show you graphs of them. Light intensity: in light, stomata open to allow CO₂ in for photosynthesis, which also lets water out — so more light = more transpiration. Temperature: warmer air means water molecules have more energy and evaporate faster. Humidity: if the air is already full of water vapour, the concentration gradient between inside the leaf and outside is smaller — so water diffuses out more slowly. Wind speed: wind carries water vapour away from the leaf surface, steepening the concentration gradient and speeding evaporation up.
writeBullets: Light intensity ↑ → stomata open → transpiration rate ↑
Temperature ↑ → faster evaporation → transpiration rate ↑
Humidity ↑ → smaller water vapour gradient → transpiration rate ↓
Wind speed ↑ → water vapour removed faster → transpiration rate ↑

keyTerms.def: The difference in concentration of a substance between two areas — the steeper the gradient, the faster diffusion
The amount of water vapour in the air

keyPoints.memTip: Three factors INCREASE transpiration (Light, Temperature, Wind) and one DECREASES it (Humidity — the odd one out). Acronym: LTW-H. Practise sketching all four graphs: three go up in a straight line, and humidity goes down. AQA will give you one and ask you to explain it.

keyPoints.heading: Leaf adaptations that reduce water loss
keyPoints.content: Plants in dry environments can't afford to lose water through the transpiration stream as quickly. Several adaptations help. The waxy cuticle on the upper surface of the leaf is waterproof — water cannot evaporate through it. The stomata are mainly on the underside of the leaf, away from direct sunlight (which would speed evaporation). At night, when there's no light for photosynthesis, the stomata close — cutting off water loss entirely. Guard cells control whether stomata are open or closed: they become turgid (fill with water) to open the stoma, and flaccid (lose water) to close it.
writeBullets: Waxy cuticle on upper leaf surface — waterproof layer, prevents evaporation
Stomata are mainly on the underside of the leaf — less exposed to direct sunlight
Stomata close in the dark — no photosynthesis, so no CO₂ needed, water loss is minimised
Guard cells control stomata: turgid (swollen with water) = open; flaccid (water lost) = closed

keyTerms.def: A waterproof layer on the outer surface of leaves that reduces water loss by evaporation
Cells that surround each stoma and control whether it is open or closed
A cell swollen with water — when guard cells are turgid, the stomata open
A cell that has lost water and is limp — when guard cells are flaccid, the stomata close

keyPoints.memTip: Guard cell memory trick: Turgid = open (T for Two guard cells bow outward like a smile, opening the gap). Flaccid = closed (F for shut Flat). AQA can ask you to describe how stomata open and close — make sure you mention both the guard cell shape change AND whether they're turgid or flaccid.

keyPoints.heading: Transpiration vs translocation — never mix these up
keyPoints.content: These two words look similar but describe completely different processes, and AQA markers see them confused constantly. Transpiration is the movement of water up through the xylem — always upward, always water. Translocation is the movement of dissolved sugars (and other solutes) through the phloem — and crucially it can go both up and down, wherever the plant needs food. Sugar made in the leaves might go down to the roots for storage, or up to growing shoot tips. Both xylem and phloem run alongside each other in vascular bundles.
writeBullets: Transpiration: water moves UP through xylem (one direction only)
Translocation: dissolved sugars move through phloem (both directions — up and down)
Xylem: dead hollow cells; carries water and mineral ions
Phloem: living cells; carries sugars (mainly sucrose) made by photosynthesis

keyTerms.def: The movement of dissolved sugars through the phloem, from where they are made to where they are needed — in both directions
Living tissue that transports dissolved sugars up and down the plant

keyPoints.memTip: X for xylem, X for water (neither has an E in the key part — both are 'dry' sounding). Ph for phloem, Ph for phood (food — sugars). Or use a table: draw two columns, label one Xylem and one Phloem, and fill in: substance carried, direction, cell type (dead vs living). That table takes 60 seconds to draw and gets you the marks.

keyPoints.heading: Graph questions — what AQA actually shows you
keyPoints.content: In the exam, you'll often be given a graph showing how one of the four factors affects transpiration rate and asked to describe or explain it. The key pattern: temperature, light and wind all give a graph that rises steadily (linear increase) — as each increases, transpiration speeds up. Humidity is the opposite: as humidity increases, the transpiration rate falls. When explaining any graph, always link to the concentration gradient: a steeper gradient means faster diffusion of water vapour out of the leaf.
writeBullets: Temperature ↑: transpiration rate increases linearly — molecules have more energy, evaporate faster
Light ↑: transpiration rate increases — stomata open wider, more water can escape
Wind speed ↑: transpiration rate increases — water vapour swept away, gradient steepened
Humidity ↑: transpiration rate decreases — smaller water vapour gradient between leaf and air
keyPoints.memTip: Sketch all four graphs from memory right now on a scrap of paper — x-axis = factor, y-axis = transpiration rate. Temperature, light, wind: lines go up. Humidity: line goes down. Once you've drawn them four times, the shapes stick. The exam gives you the axes; you add the line and the explanation.

tables.title: Transpiration vs translocation
rows: Active loading of sugar into phloem

tables.title: The four factors and their effect on transpiration rate
rows: Molecules have more energy; evaporation faster
Water vapour gradient between leaf and air is smaller
Water vapour removed from leaf surface; gradient steepened

1.question: What pulls water up through the xylem in the transpiration stream?
options: The heart pumping blood around the plant
Active transport by living xylem cells
Evaporation of water from leaves through stomata, which pulls more water up
Root pressure pushing water up from below
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Water evaporates out of the stomata and that loss pulls more water up through the xylem from the roots. No pump needed — just physics.
1.wrongFeedback: The pull comes from evaporation at the top. As water leaves through the stomata, it drags the water molecules below it upward through the xylem. Xylem cells are dead and hollow — they don't actively pump anything.

3.question: A student increases the humidity around a plant. What happens to the transpiration rate, and why?
options: Transpiration rate increases — more water vapour in the air speeds up evaporation
Transpiration rate decreases — the water vapour concentration gradient between the leaf and the air becomes smaller
Transpiration rate stays the same — humidity has no effect on stomata
Transpiration rate increases — stomata open wider in high humidity
3.correctFeedback: Correct. High humidity means the air is already full of water vapour, so the gradient between inside the leaf and outside is smaller — water diffuses out more slowly.
3.wrongFeedback: Humidity is the odd one out among the four factors — it's the only one that decreases transpiration rate. When humidity is high, the concentration gradient between the humid air inside the leaf and the air outside is small, so water diffuses out more slowly.

commonMistakes: Xylem carries water upward only. Phloem carries sugars in both directions. Mixing these up loses marks on almost every transpiration question.

Stomata close in the dark — not because the plant 'rests', but because there is no light for photosynthesis and therefore no need to take in CO₂. Water loss is the cost of having stomata open; the plant avoids that cost when there's nothing to gain.
Humidity is the only factor that decreases transpiration rate. The other three (light, temperature, wind) all increase it. If you write 'humidity increases transpiration', you will lose the mark.

Xylem cells are dead — they are hollow tubes with no living contents, which is exactly what makes them good at carrying water. If a question asks why xylem is suited to its function, 'dead hollow cells' is the answer.
The transpiration stream moves water and mineral ions, not sugars. Sugars are moved by translocation through the phloem.
examTips: For any graph question on transpiration: describe the trend first ('as X increases, transpiration rate increases'), then explain WHY using concentration gradient and stomata.
If asked to compare xylem and phloem: four things — substance carried, direction, cell type (dead vs living), and process name (transpiration vs translocation).
Guard cell questions always need two things: the shape change (turgid vs flaccid) AND the outcome (stomata open vs closed).
revisionCardBullets: Transpiration stream: water enters roots by osmosis → moves up xylem → evaporates out of stomata
Xylem = dead hollow cells; carries water + mineral ions; upward only
Phloem = living cells; carries dissolved sugars; both directions (translocation)
Light ↑: transpiration ↑ (stomata open) | Temperature ↑: transpiration ↑ (faster evaporation)
Humidity ↑: transpiration ↓ (smaller gradient) | Wind ↑: transpiration ↑ (removes water vapour)
Waxy cuticle = waterproof layer on upper leaf surface — reduces water loss
Stomata mainly on underside of leaf — less direct sunlight, reduces water loss
Stomata close in the dark — no photosynthesis needed, water loss minimised
Guard cells: turgid = stomata open | flaccid = stomata closed
Transpiration = water, xylem, up | Translocation = sugars, phloem, both directions
